repo_notes.content: Vault Cortex is a remote MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that gives any AI agent hybrid search, task management, structured memory, and read/write access to an Obsidian vault over HTTPS. It runs headless in Docker, working directly with .md files on disk using its own Obsidian-parity parsers and search index. Key capabilities: a hybrid search engine (SQLite FTS5 keyword search + sqlite-vec vector KNN, fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion and optionally reranked by a cross-encoder with position-aware score blending), a full Obsidian link resolution engine covering every link format Obsidian recognizes, Kanban-aware task management (query with structured filters plus one-call completion, reprioritization, and lane moves — parsing both Tasks plugin emoji and Dataview inline-field formats), a heading-aware structured memory layer for AI personalization, and OAuth 2.1 authentication with JWT tokens. Tools and user-initiated prompts, with tool descriptions optimized for agent efficiency using Glama's TDQS (Tool Description Quality Score) model. Deploy on a VPS with Obsidian Sync and the vault is accessible from your phone, claude.ai, or any remote MCP client. The project maintains a comprehensive security posture: runtime hardening (atomic writes with TOCTOU guards, path traversal prevention via resolveSafePath, per-file write mutex with three concurrency modes, FTS5 query sanitization, prompt injection defense with tag-breakout escaping, memory layer shrink/idempotency guards), Docker container hardening (non-root user, tini PID 1, package-manager removal, digest-pinned base), and automated scanning (OpenSSF Scorecard, Trivy container scanning, Gitleaks secret detection, Dependabot dependency updates, CodeQL static analysis, Socket Security supply chain monitoring, Snyk vulnerability scanning). Listed on the official MCP server registry and awesome-mcp-servers (https://github.com/punkpeye/awesome-mcp-servers). A companion obsidian-vault skill teaches agents Obsidian-specific editing conventions (frontmatter, wikilinks, callouts, plugin compatibility) so the content Vault Cortex delivers renders correctly. Deployment ranges from a single Docker container with a bind-mounted vault folder to a full AWS reference deployment with API Gateway, Lambda authorizer, and Obsidian Sync. The repo also ships a CLI (cli/ directory, published as the vault-cortex npm package) that scaffolds a deployment in one command: npx vault-cortex@latest init.

The codebase is organized into four internal layers under src/vault-mcp/: (1) obsidian-markdown/ — pure Obsidian/Markdown parsers with no I/O: link grammar, extraction, and resolution for all of Obsidian's link formats (wikilinks, markdown links, embeds, frontmatter links, relative paths); fence-aware line classification; frontmatter parsing; callout extraction; heading section parsing; plaintext stripping for embedding. (2) vault-operations/ — filesystem I/O for vault content: CRUD, surgical heading-targeted patching, note moving with vault-wide link rewriting, the About Me/ memory store, and daily note resolution. (3) search/ — SQLite FTS5 + sqlite-vec search index with heading-aware chunking, local ONNX embedding pipeline (bge-small-en-v1.5, INT8 quantized), content-hash gating for incremental re-embedding, and chokidar file watcher that keeps both the FTS index and vector embeddings current on every file change. The embedding pipeline is opt-out via EMBEDDING_ENABLED. (4) mcp-core/ — the MCP protocol surface: streamable-http transport, tool registrations split into domain group modules under mcp-core/tools/ (vault-crud, search, memory, daily-note — each self-contained with its own register function and tool name constants; the orchestrator in tool-definitions.ts composes them and conditionally skips memory tools when MEMORY_ENABLED=false), and prompt definitions. Authentication lives in src/vault-mcp/oauth/ (OAuth 2.1 with JWT tokens) and src/functions/authorizer.ts (AWS Lambda, path-aware). Infrastructure-as-code (sst.config.ts) is specific to the AWS reference deployment and should not be documented as the primary deployment path.

pages.title: Overview
pages.purpose: What Vault Cortex does: gives any AI agent hybrid search, task management, structured memory, and full read/write access to an Obsidian vault — a full tool suite and guided prompts over HTTPS. Key capabilities: hybrid search (FTS5 keyword + vector KNN, fused via RRF with optional cross-encoder reranking), Kanban-aware task management (query with structured filters across 6 date fields plus one-call completion, reprioritization, and lane moves — parsing both Tasks plugin emoji and Dataview inline-field formats; done-lane auto-detection on Kanban boards), a heading-aware memory layer for AI personalization, OAuth 2.1 authentication, and TDQS-optimized tool descriptions. Runs headless in Docker with its own Obsidian-parity parsers and search index. Three deployment modes (local Docker, remote with Obsidian Sync, AWS reference) and a comprehensive security toolchain (OpenSSF Scorecard, Trivy, Gitleaks, Dependabot, CodeQL, Socket Security, Snyk). Listed on the official MCP server registry and awesome-mcp-servers.

pages.title: Getting Started
pages.purpose: Quickstart guide covering the three deployment paths (local Docker, remote with Obsidian Sync, AWS reference) with links to the deploy/ directory walkthroughs. Includes MCP client configuration examples for Claude Desktop, Claude Code, and other clients. Highlights the npx vault-cortex init CLI as the fastest path to a working setup.

pages.title: CLI
pages.purpose: The vault-cortex npm package (cli/) — a scaffolding tool that sets up a Vault Cortex deployment in one command: npx vault-cortex@latest init. Covers: the interactive init flow (mode selection, vault path, token generation, docker-compose.yml and .env scaffolding, optional docker compose up with health-check wait); non-interactive flags (--mode, --vault-path, --dir, --yes); the module structure (bin.ts entry point, main.ts top-level wiring, program.ts command definition, init.ts orchestration, prompts.ts interactive flow, scaffold.ts file generation, docker.ts container management, env.ts environment file handling, token.ts secure token generation, vault.ts path validation, node-version.ts compatibility check, messages.ts user-facing output); and the templates/ directory used for scaffolding.

pages.title: Configuration Reference
pages.purpose: All environment variables (from config.ts and .env.example files), the ServerConfig type, and how configuration differs across the three deployment modes. Covers VAULT_PATH, MCP_AUTH_TOKEN, OBSIDIAN_AUTH_TOKEN, VAULT_NAME, LOG_LEVEL, EMBEDDING_ENABLED (opt-out: disables the ONNX embedding pipeline, vector tables, and embedding passes when false — search uses FTS5 only), RERANK_MODE (blended or none: controls cross-encoder reranking after RRF fusion, only takes effect when EMBEDDING_ENABLED is true), MEMORY_ENABLED (opt-out: disables memory tools, bootstrap, and server metadata references when false), MEMORY_DIR, DAILY_NOTES_FOLDER, DAILY_NOTES_FORMAT, and Windows bind-mount mode.

pages.title: Architecture
pages.purpose: Parent page providing an overview of the four-layer internal architecture (obsidian-markdown, vault-operations, search, mcp-core), the data flow from MCP client request through transport to data layer and back, and the component diagram showing how Obsidian Sync, the vault filesystem, SQLite, and the MCP server interact.

pages.title: Server and Transport
pages.purpose: How the Express server bootstraps (server.ts), the MCP streamable-http transport lifecycle (mcp-core/mcp-router.ts), session management, and the logger chain that flows context (sessionId, clientIp, requestId, tool) through child loggers.

pages.title: Authentication and Security
pages.purpose: The two-layer auth model: AWS Lambda authorizer (path-aware — OAuth endpoints pass through, /mcp validates static token or JWT) and the OAuth 2.1 provider (oauth-provider.ts with JWT tokens and SQLite persistence, consent page, SDK auth routes). Covers src/auth.ts (safeEqual timing-safe comparison, parseBearer), src/jwt.ts (HS256 sign/verify with timingSafeEqual), and the security policy. Beyond auth, covers the full runtime hardening posture documented in ARCHITECTURE.md Data Integrity and SECURITY.md Runtime Hardening: path traversal prevention (resolveSafePath, toVaultRelativePath normalization), atomic writes with TOCTOU guards (link-based no-clobber, preflight-then-commit moves), SQL/prompt injection prevention (parameterized queries, sanitizeFtsQuery, tag-breakout escaping), error boundary with info-leak prevention (safeHandler, path masking), memory layer safety guards (shrink, idempotency, ambiguity), and Docker container hardening (non-root, tini, package-manager removal, digest-pinned base).

pages.title: Obsidian Markdown Parsing
pages.purpose: The pure parser layer in obsidian-markdown/ — no filesystem I/O, just domain knowledge about the Obsidian/Markdown format. Covers: links.ts (link grammar, fence-aware extraction, resolution for wikilinks, markdown links, embeds, frontmatter links, and relative path-from-current-file links — all three of Obsidian's 'New link format' modes); lines.ts (CRLF-safe line splitting, the consolidated fence state machine, classifyLines for identifying fenced regions); frontmatter.ts (gray-matter parse/stringify, frontmatter merge); callouts.ts (leading-callout parser for > [!type] blocks); headings.ts (shared H1-H6 section-span parser used by both read and patch operations); tasks.ts (Tasks-plugin task-line grammar covering emoji signifiers and Dataview inline fields); memory-entries.ts (memory-entry grammar — parses the dated-bullet format of About Me/ memory files into individual entries for vault_memory_recall's entry-granular index); plaintext.ts (strips Obsidian/Markdown syntax to produce plain text for the embedding pipeline). This layer is architecturally significant — it encodes the Obsidian-parity behavior that distinguishes Vault Cortex from simpler file-based integrations.

page_notes.content: The link resolution engine in links.ts is one of the project's key differentiators. It handles every link format Obsidian recognizes: [[wikilink]], [[wikilink|alias]], [[wikilink#heading]], ![[embed]], [markdown](path.md), links in frontmatter properties (e.g. related:), and all three 'New link format' modes (shortest path, path from vault folder, path from current file including relative ../ paths). The fence state machine was consolidated from three separate implementations into one shared classifyLines function in lines.ts during a recent restructure.

pages.title: Vault Operations
pages.purpose: The filesystem I/O layer in vault-operations/ that reads, writes, and transforms vault content. Safety primitives: atomic writes (atomicWriteFile temp-then-rename, atomicWriteFileExclusive link-based no-clobber for TOCTOU-safe creates), per-file write locking (file-write-lock.ts — serializing, fail-fast, and multi-file fail-fast modes), path traversal prevention (resolveSafePath resolve + prefix check, toVaultRelativePath normalization), and protected-path enforcement (PROTECTED_PATHS checked after normalization). Covers: vault-filesystem.ts (read/write/list/delete .md files, outline and section reads, protected-path validation); vault-patcher.ts (surgical edits — heading-targeted patch with prepend/append/replace operations, and find-and-replace with optional replace-all); note-mover.ts (move/rename a note and rewrite every vault-wide link that pointed to it, using the link resolution engine from obsidian-markdown/links.ts — preflight-then-commit design with multi-file exclusive locking); memory-store.ts (About Me/ heading-aware read/append/delete with shrink guard, idempotency guard, and ambiguity guard — see Memory Layer page); daily-notes.ts (daily note config reader and path resolver — see Memory Layer page). All functions follow the (params, logger) two-argument pattern.

pages.title: Search and Indexing
pages.purpose: The SQLite FTS5 + sqlite-vec search system in search/, decomposed across nine modules. search-index.ts is the factory/closure (createSearchIndex) — schema, migrations, prepared statements, write operations (upsertNote, embedNote, removeNote, rebuildFromVault), non-md file tracking, and context wiring that binds query functions to the db instance. search-queries.ts holds all 17 query methods (fullTextSearch, hybridSearch, memoryRecall, searchByTag, searchByFolder, listTasks, recentNotes, listPropertyKeys, listPropertyValues, searchByProperty, getBacklinks, getOutgoingLinks, findOrphans, brokenLinkCount, modifiedOnDate, vaultStats, listAllTags) as named exports taking a SearchQueryContext. search-helpers.ts contains pure data transforms — row mappers (rowToMetadata, rowToTaskEntry, noteRowToSearchResult), filters (noteMatchesSearchFilters), type guards (isString, coerceToArray), and utilities (buildFtsMetadataText, mtimeToIso, buildSnippetFromChunkText, escapeLikeWildcards, stripTrailingSlashes). fts-query.ts encapsulates FTS5 query sanitization (sanitizeFtsQuery). rrf.ts implements Reciprocal Rank Fusion scoring (computeRrfScores, k=60 with top-rank bonuses). reranker.ts is the cross-encoder reranker factory (createReranker, lazy-loading ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 INT8 quantized) plus position-aware score blending (blendScores, normalizeScores) — after RRF fusion, the reranker rescores top candidates by query-document interaction and blends with RRF scores using rank-dependent weights (75/25 at top ranks, 40/60 at lower ranks), controlled by RERANK_MODE. The embedding pipeline — embedder.ts (lazy-loading ONNX pipeline for bge-small-en-v1.5 with singleton guard against concurrent model downloads), chunker.ts (heading-aware chunking with paragraph sub-splitting for oversized sections and title prefixing), and content-hash gating (SHA-256 per chunk) that skips re-embedding unchanged content on both incremental updates and full rebuilds. Memory files additionally get an entry-granular index (memory_entries + FTS + cosine vec0 vectors, reconciled by content-hash identity so an append re-embeds exactly one entry) powering the memoryRecall query behind vault_memory_recall — see the Memory Layer page. A non_md_files table tracks non-markdown vault files (.canvas, .base, images, PDFs) so wikilinks to assets resolve as kind: 'asset' instead of counting as broken. file-watcher.ts (chokidar watcher that keeps the FTS index, vector embeddings, and non_md_files table current on every file add/change/unlink, with per-path promise serialization for embedding and Windows polling mode support). Hybrid search runs FTS5 keyword search then vector KNN sequentially, fuses results via RRF, optionally reranks via the cross-encoder with position-aware blending, and falls back to FTS-only silently when vectors are unavailable. The vault_search tool description is conditional on EMBEDDING_ENABLED — hybrid-aware when true, FTS-only when false. The task index is a dedicated tasks table storing every checkbox task line, parsed by obsidian-markdown/tasks.ts — a faithful reimplementation of the Tasks plugin's parser supporting both emoji signifiers and Dataview inline-field formats. Kanban-aware: each task carries is_kanban_task (detected via kanban-plugin frontmatter), heading (the Kanban lane), and full note attribution. vault_list_tasks queries the table with structured filters (status as string or array, 6 date fields with before/on/after bounds, priority, folder, tag, heading as string or array, path) and 8 sort keys including date cascade sorting (fallback through related date fields with per-field direction) and position sorting for Kanban board order.

pages.title: Memory Layer
pages.purpose: The About Me/ memory system and daily note support. Covers: memory-store.ts (heading-aware read/append/delete for structured memory files — each file has H2 sections with dated bullet entries, supporting get/update/delete by file and section); daily-notes.ts (reads daily note configuration from the vault's .obsidian/daily-notes.json and resolves the path for any date using Luxon); templates/memory/ (bootstrap templates for new vaults). The memory layer enables personalized AI interactions — agents read user preferences, principles, and context from About Me/ files. vault_memory_recall adds entry-granular hybrid retrieval over the layer: dated entries are parsed individually (obsidian-markdown/memory-entries.ts) into dedicated FTS and cosine-vector index tables with content-hash reconciliation, then recalled oldest-first so the evolution of a preference or opinion reads in order — lexical matching always works, vectors and the cross-encoder relevance cut apply when embeddings are enabled. The entire layer is opt-out: set MEMORY_ENABLED=false to hide all memory tools and the memory-review prompt, skip auto-initialization, and strip memory references from server metadata.

pages.title: MCP Interface
pages.purpose: Parent page for the MCP protocol surface — the tools and prompts that clients interact with. Overview of the tools (grouped by domain: vault CRUD, search and discovery, tasks, memory, graph queries, properties, daily notes) and the user-initiated prompts. Explains the distinction: tools are model-driven (the AI decides when to call them), prompts are user-initiated (surfaced as slash commands or menu items in the client). Both tools and prompts surface live vault content — prompts assemble real-time vault data (folder structure, tags, property keys, memory files, recent notes, daily notes) at invocation time, not static instructions.

pages.title: Tool Reference
pages.purpose: The MCP tools, now organized in domain group modules under mcp-core/tools/: vault-crud-tools.ts (read, write, patch, replace, delete-span, list, delete, move, update-properties), search-tools.ts (search, search-by-tag, search-by-folder, recent-notes, list-tags, list-property-keys, list-property-values, search-by-property, get-backlinks, get-outgoing-links, find-orphans), task-tools.ts (list-tasks, update-task), memory-tools.ts (get-memory, update-memory, list-memory-files, delete-memory, memory-recall; conditionally registered when MEMORY_ENABLED=true), and daily-note-tools.ts (get-daily-note). vault_list_tasks and vault_update_task in task-tools.ts provide Kanban-aware task management: vault_list_tasks is a vault-wide task index with structured filters (status, 6 date fields, priority, folder/tag/heading/path scope), array params for multi-lane queries, date cascade sorting, and position sorting for board order; vault_update_task applies status, priority, and/or lane changes in a single call — marking done auto-detects the done lane (via **Complete** markers, falling back to 'Done' heading), stamps/removes completion dates, and writes in the user's configured Tasks plugin format (emoji or Dataview). The orchestrator in tool-definitions.ts composes TOOL_NAMES from each group and calls register functions. Each tool description includes Example, When to use, Returns, and Errors sections — optimized against Glama's TDQS (Tool Description Quality Score) model to ensure agents select and invoke the right tool efficiently.

pages.title: Prompt Reference
pages.purpose: The 3 MCP prompts, organized in group modules under mcp-core/prompts/ (mirroring the tools/ pattern) with prompt-definitions.ts as the orchestrator — each queries the search index, link graph, and memory layer at invocation time, assembling live vault data with guided instructions. vault-orientation (zero-arg: vault stats with total notes/untagged/broken links, folder note counts, property adoption rates with low-adoption flags, orphan detection via findOrphans, tag cloud, recent notes, memory outline, and contextual tool suggestions based on findings); memory-review (structural overview from listMemoryFiles showing scope callouts, section entry counts, and file sizes before the raw content; reads memory as an append-only evolution (files marked entry-policy: living are current-state snapshots whose expired entries may be pruned); guided reflection adds scope-fit against declared scopes and coverage analysis for gaps); daily-review (outgoing links via getOutgoingLinks with broken-link flags, backlinks via getBacklinks, date-specific activity via modifiedOnDate instead of global recentNotes so past-date reviews show that date's work; guides reconciliation, link following, and pattern recognition). Prompts degrade gracefully — an empty memory layer or missing daily note returns a valid message with getting-started guidance, never a hard failure. Autocomplete is supported on the memory-review file argument.

pages.title: Deployment
pages.purpose: Parent page for the three deployment modes that enable access to your vault from anywhere. All modes run the vault-cortex Docker image, built from one two-target Dockerfile: the :latest tag is the MCP server alone (local mode), and the :remote tag bundles Obsidian Sync in the same container under s6-overlay supervision — a single OCI image that runs on any container runtime (docker run, Podman, nerdctl) with no Compose requirement. The modes progress from local-only to fully remote: local Docker (bind-mounted vault, no network), remote Docker with Obsidian Sync (access from any device), and the AWS reference deployment (defense-in-depth security with API Gateway, Lambda authorizer, and OAuth 2.1). The local and remote modes have comprehensive quickstart guides in deploy/ and can be scaffolded by the CLI (npx vault-cortex init --mode local or --mode remote). The AWS mode is a reference architecture used in production by the author, with its own SST IaC and deployment guide (DEPLOY.md).

pages.title: Local Docker Deployment
pages.purpose: The simplest deployment: a single Docker container with the vault folder bind-mounted from the host. No Obsidian Sync, no cloud services. Documented in deploy/local/. Covers the docker-compose.yml, .env.example (just MCP_AUTH_TOKEN and VAULT_PATH), and MCP client configuration. Includes Windows bind-mount support (WINDOWS_MODE flag for polling watcher and exclusive-write strategy).

pages.title: Remote Deployment with Obsidian Sync
pages.purpose: One container from the vault-cortex:remote image: s6-overlay supervises both the bidirectional Obsidian Sync process (obsidian-headless CLI, with an init chain handling login, vault setup, and PUID/PGID mapping — service definitions live in rootfs/) and the MCP server, with startup ordering between them. Named Docker volumes, no bind mounts; runs via docker compose or plain docker run. Documented in deploy/remote/. Requires an Obsidian Sync subscription and HTTPS (reverse proxy or cloud provider). This is the deployment that enables mobile/remote access — the key differentiator over local-only solutions.

pages.title: AWS Reference Deployment
pages.purpose: The full reference deployment using SST v4 infrastructure-as-code with a defense-in-depth security posture: Lightsail container service (~$12–24/mo) running the vault-cortex:remote image as a single Compose service, API Gateway HTTP API for HTTPS with automatic TLS, and a Lambda authorizer (path-aware: OAuth endpoints pass through, /mcp validates static token or JWT). Defense in depth: TLS termination at API Gateway, token validation at the Lambda authorizer layer, and OAuth 2.1 with JWT at the application layer — three independent security boundaries. Covers sst.config.ts, the Lightsail Docker Compose setup, and DEPLOY.md. This is the author's production deployment but is not required — Vault Cortex runs anywhere Docker does.

pages.title: Development Guide
pages.purpose: Contributing to Vault Cortex: local development setup (Node.js via nvm, npm run dev, docker-compose.local.yml for building from source), the test suite (vitest with table-driven tests and behavioral spec naming), the structured JSON logging system (logger.ts with auto-captured source locations and the child-logger context chain), release conventions (conventional commits, automated changelog, GitHub Actions CI/CD), and the security toolchain (OpenSSF Scorecard, Trivy container scanning, Gitleaks secret detection, Dependabot dependency updates, CodeQL static analysis, Socket Security supply chain monitoring, Snyk vulnerability scanning). References CONTRIBUTING.md.
